title: Dog Proptosis: Emergency Guide for an Eyeball Out of Socket
meta_desc: Dog proptosis is a critical emergency. Learn what to do if your dog's eye is out of the socket and why immediate veterinary care is essential.
content_html:
What is Ocular Proptosis?

Ocular proptosis occurs when a dog's eyeball is displaced forward, protruding out of the bony eye socket. When this happens, the eyelids often become trapped behind the globe, preventing the dog from blinking and causing the surface of the eye to dry out rapidly. This condition is most commonly seen in brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Pugs, Shih Tzus, and Bulldogs due to their shallow eye sockets, usually following trauma or a scuffle with another animal.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for proptosis is High. This is a severe, sight-threatening emergency that requires immediate medical intervention. The displacement causes significant stretching of the optic nerve and can tear the muscles and blood vessels surrounding the eye. To have any chance of saving your dog's vision or even the eye itself, you must seek emergency veterinary care within minutes, not hours.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are able to safely take a quick, clear photo of the eye without delaying your departure, it can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary triage team. Sending a photo ahead of your arrival allows the staff to determine the severity of the swelling, check for globe rupture, and prepare the necessary surgical suite and pain management protocols. This preparation ensures that your dog receives life-saving treatment the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Should I try to push the eye back in myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to push the eyeball back into the socket yourself. Doing so can cause permanent damage to the optic nerve, increase internal eye pressure, and cause your dog extreme pain.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the eye moist by gently covering it with a clean, soft cloth soaked in warm water or sterile saline. Do not apply any pressure to the eye and try to keep your dog calm to prevent further swelling.

faqs.question: Can a dog's eye be saved after proptosis?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the breed and the severity of the injury. While many dogs may lose vision in the affected eye, rapid surgical intervention can often save the physical eye itself for cosmetic and comfort reasons.
